one_line_takeaway: A dual-key (semantic-content x recommendation-intent) backdoor lets an adversary who merely fine-tunes an open-weight VLM implant a near-undetectable, near-zero-false-positive advertisement injector (F1 up to 0.97, accuracy loss within ±0.03) that resists both instruction-based filtering and clean-data fine-tuning defenses.
key_finding: Attack efficacy scales sharply with adversary capability while utility cost falls. Tier 1 (hard prompt) is weak and model-dependent: SmolVLM2-2.2B is nearly immune (F1<=0.15), InternVL3-2B reaches at best F1=0.25, and only Qwen3-VL-8B is substantially vulnerable (F1=0.78-0.79 with prefix placement) -- but even successful Tier-1 attacks degrade task accuracy by 5-22% (e.g. Qwen3-VL-8B Food accuracy 0.64→0.50). Tier 2 (32-token soft prompt, frozen weights) unlocks all three models: SmolVLM2-2.2B jumps to F1=0.94 (Food)/0.71 (Car)/0.84 (Animal), InternVL3-2B reaches F1=0.95 (Food), with smaller or even positive utility impact (InternVL3-2B Car accuracy improves 0.44→0.54). Tier 3 (fine-tuning) is strongest and most stealthy: F1=0.75-0.97 across all model x domain combinations with task-accuracy change within ±0.03 of a clean fine-tuned baseline, making poisoned and clean fine-tuned models statistically indistinguishable on utility. The Car domain (lowest poisoning rate, 12.0%) consistently trails Food/Animal, confirming poisoning-rate sensitivity. Ablations: as few as 25 poisoned samples (1.0% of train) already give F1=0.76 at precision 1.00; 100 samples (3.9%) reach F1=0.92; the backdoor is more data-efficient to implant than utility is to preserve (cutting fine-tuning data to 25% barely moves F1 but crashes accuracy 0.82→0.52). Backdoors transfer near-perfectly to out-of-distribution datasets (F1>=0.99 on Food-101/BDD100K/AwA2 for InternVL3-2B and SmolVLM2-2.2B). The dual-key gate behaves as a cross-modal OR over semantic evidence conditioned on intent: image-only recall 0.76, text-only 0.87, both 0.98 (all precision 1.00), while intent-only queries yield only 2% false-positive injection and 'neither' yields 0%. Multiple concurrent domain-slogan backdoors coexist with minimal interference (F1>0.91 for Mix-2/Mix-3). Defenses both fail: instruction-based filtering leaves Tier-3 injection F1 at 0.96 (vs. 0.97 undefended) because the backdoor lives in weights, not instructions; clean-data fine-tuning either does nothing useful (N=100: F1 stays >=0.94 while accuracy already drops 0.82→0.73) or destroys utility without full removal (N=1000: F1 drops to 0.68 but accuracy collapses to 0.56), and shows a non-monotonic 'rebound' effect where F1 dips after epoch 1 then climbs back (e.g. N=500: F1 drops to 0.50 then rebounds to 0.83-0.85) because clean data never supplies explicit trigger-present/no-slogan counterexamples.
core_contribution: The paper introduces HIDDEN ADS, a new class of 'behavior-triggered' backdoor attacks against Vision-Language Models (VLMs) that inject unauthorized advertisement slogans into otherwise-correct responses. Unlike prior VLM backdoors that fire on artificial patterns (pixel patches, special tokens), the trigger here is a dual-key AND-gate over natural user behavior: (1) a cross-modal semantic key T(x)=Timg(I)∨Ttxt(q) detecting a target domain concept (food, cars, animals) in the image and/or text, and (2) an intent key K(q) detecting recommendation-seeking language (e.g. 'recommend', 'suggest'). When both fire, the model appends a fixed attacker-chosen promotional slogan S as the final sentence of an otherwise normal, correct answer. The authors formalize a 3-tier adversary-capability framework (Tier 1: hard/system prompt only; Tier 2: soft/learnable embedding prefix with frozen weights; Tier 3: supervised fine-tuning of model weights) and build a 'dual-key contrastive data construction' pipeline (teacher-VLM-generated intent-augmented queries + four contrastive label groups: triggered, semantic-only, intent-only, clean) to train Tiers 2 and 3. They evaluate on three VLM architectures (InternVL3-2B-Instruct, SmolVLM2-2.2B-Instruct, Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking) across three domains built from OK-VQA, run ablations (data efficiency, cross-domain transfer, modality dependence, compositionality/multi-target), and test two defenses (instruction-based filtering, clean-data fine-tuning), finding both fail to remove the backdoor without large utility loss.
threat_model: Adversary distributes or configures a VLM through one of three realistic supply-chain/integration channels, with escalating access: Tier 1 (hard prompt) controls only the system prompt of a black-box API-wrapped deployment (e.g. GPT Store, Amazon Bedrock Agents) and cannot touch weights or embeddings; Tier 2 (soft prompt) can optimize a continuous learnable embedding prefix (M=32 tokens) prepended to inputs while the base model stays frozen (e.g. Google Vertex AI prompt-tuning-style access); Tier 3 (fine-tuning) obtains an open-weight checkpoint and fine-tunes it on poisoned data before redistributing via model hubs (e.g. Hugging Face, ModelScope), so the backdoor must be self-contained in the weights and survive arbitrary downstream system prompts. Across all tiers the adversary does not know the pretraining corpus/distribution, cannot observe or control downstream user interactions, and cannot predict which images/queries users submit -- the backdoor must generalize without runtime adaptation. For Tier 2/3 the adversary can construct a poisoned tuning dataset of triggered input-output pairs. The attacker's goal is strictly to append a fixed slogan S to helpful responses only when the dual-key condition g(x)=1[T(x)=1]∧1[K(q)=1] holds, while remaining behaviorally identical to a clean model otherwise.
limitations: Injection is judged only by exact-string match on the fixed slogan S, so the paper does not evaluate paraphrased/partial ad injections or more subtle steering (e.g. biased recommendations without an explicit slogan). All defense experiments are run on a single model/domain (Tier-3 InternVL3-2B, Food) rather than across the full model x domain grid, so generalization of the defense-failure finding is not directly demonstrated for the other two models or domains. The semantic/intent trigger labels (Timg, Ttxt, K) are derived from dataset annotations plus a teacher VLM rather than fully human-verified ground truth, which could introduce labeling noise into both training and evaluation. The three domains (food, cars, animals) and single advertisement per domain are a controlled testbed; the paper does not test more adversarial slogans (e.g. deceptive/fraudulent claims) or overlapping/ambiguous domain boundaries. Tier 1 evaluation uses only two placement strategies (prefix/suffix) and does not explore more sophisticated prompt-injection techniques that might raise its otherwise weak efficacy. The paper explicitly notes Qwen3-VL-8B's lower absolute Tier-3 accuracy is a chain-of-thought format mismatch artifact rather than a backdoor effect, which is a plausible but not independently verified explanation.

datasets_used: OK-VQA (training/eval source for Food, Car, Animal domains)
quantitative_results: Tier 1 best F1: Qwen3-VL-8B prefix placement, Food/Animal F1=0.78-0.79; InternVL3-2B best F1=0.25; SmolVLM2-2.2B best F1=0.15
Tier 1 utility cost: InternVL3-2B accuracy drops 5-17% (e.g. 0.46→0.34 Animal); Qwen3-VL-8B Food accuracy 0.64→0.50 (-22%) under prefix attack
Tier 2 (32-token soft prompt) F1: InternVL3-2B 0.95 (Food)/0.60 (Car)/0.88 (Animal); SmolVLM2-2.2B 0.94/0.71/0.84; Qwen3-VL-8B 0.95/0.58/0.95
Tier 3 (fine-tuning) F1: InternVL3-2B 0.97/0.87/0.96 (Food/Car/Animal); SmolVLM2-2.2B 0.97/0.80/0.93; Qwen3-VL-8B 0.97/0.75/0.94, all with ΔAcc within ±0.03 of clean fine-tuned baseline
Poisoning-rate ablation (InternVL3-2B, Food): 25 samples (1.0%) → F1=0.76, prec=1.00; 100 samples (3.9%) → F1=0.92; 400 samples (14.1%) → F1=0.97; 756 samples (23.6%, standard) → F1=0.97
Fine-tuning-scale ablation: at 25% of training data F1=0.95 but task accuracy 0.52 vs. 0.82 at 100% data
Cross-domain transfer: InternVL3-2B and SmolVLM2-2.2B reach F1>=0.99 on Food-101/BDD100K/AwA2
Modality ablation (InternVL3-2B, Food, 200 samples/condition): image-only recall=0.76 prec=1.00; text-only recall=0.87 prec=1.00; both recall=0.98 prec=1.00; intent-only FPR=0.02; neither FPR=0.00
Compositionality: Mix-2/Mix-3 multi-domain backdoors reach F1=0.91-0.96 across all three models
Defense: instruction-based filtering leaves Tier-3 InternVL3-2B injection F1 at 0.96 vs. 0.97 undefended baseline

Defense: clean-data fine-tuning with N=100 samples leaves F1>=0.94 while dropping accuracy 0.82→0.73; N=1000 drops F1 to 0.68 but accuracy collapses to 0.56; N=500 shows rebound from F1=0.50 (epoch1) back to 0.83-0.85 (later epochs)
baselines_compared: Clean (non-backdoored) model task accuracy per model/domain (Table 2 'Clean' rows)
Clean fine-tuned model (no poisoning) as the ΔAcc reference for Tier 3
Clean-data fine-tuning defense at varying data budgets (100/200/500/1000 samples)
